original_hypothesis.title: FeesHandlerUpgradeable base setters are publicly callable in inheritors that forget to override, enabling permissionless fee parameter hijack
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: FeesHandlerUpgradeable exposes public virtual setters (setFeeFaucet, setTxFeeRate, setGaslessBasefee) without access control. EURFToken correctly overrides them with onlyRole(ADMIN), but any future token/contract inheriting FeesHandlerUpgradeable without overriding would allow anyone to set faucet and fee rates, causing user fund loss via fee extraction or gasless basefee drains.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a common upgrade/maintenance failure mode: a future version or another deployment might reuse FeesHandlerUpgradeable directly or via a different token, forgetting to gate setters. Because this code is in-scope and reusable, it is a practical 'footgun' that can lead to production incidents.
deployment_assumptions: A production deployment uses FeesHandlerUpgradeable in a contract that does not override these setters with access control (or accidentally exposes base methods via diamond/facet/proxy wiring)
Attackers can call the exposed setters directly
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: In Token.sol, setFeeFaucet/setTxFeeRate/setGaslessBasefee are overridden with onlyRole(ADMIN). No evidence for other inheritors/deployments is provided.

proof_of_signal.pattern: public virtual setters with no access control: setFeeFaucet/setTxFeeRate/setGaslessBasefee

preconditions: A contract exposes FeesHandlerUpgradeable setters without override access control
Contract actually uses _txfeeRate/_gaslessBasefee in value-moving paths

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: missing access control in base module
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: anyone can set fee parameters leading to forced value extraction or DoS

analysis_questions: Are there any other contracts (current or planned) inheriting FeesHandlerUpgradeable besides EURFToken?
Could upgrades accidentally remove/rename the overrides, re-exposing the base setters?
Is there any selector collision risk causing base setters to be callable?
validation_requirements: Run upgrade diff analysis to ensure overrides remain present
Verify ABI of deployed token exposes only the overridden functions with correct access modifiers

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a future token forgets to override: attacker calls setFeeFaucet(attacker) and setTxFeeRate(10000). Every user transfer then pays 100% to attacker-controlled faucet, or gasless basefee is set extremely high and drained via trusted forwarder mechanics.
prerequisites: Misconfiguration or future code change (maintenance risk)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Severe: permissionless fee configuration can extract large user value or freeze transfers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the affected deployment
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium: requires upgrade/role repair; losses may be irreversible

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests shown ensuring fee setters are always access-controlled across versions/deployments.

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: monitor FeeFaucetUpdated/TxFeeRateUpdated events for unauthorized callers

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
